name: Welcome to Pi Dash 👋
description_html:
Welcome to Pi Dash — the platform that hands your work items off to AI coding agents (like Claude Code or Codex) running on machines you control. Track the work like a normal board; the agents do the typing.

This tutorial project is a safe playground. Each work item below walks through one piece of the workflow, from connecting a repo to reviewing what the agent produced. Click around freely — nothing here touches real code.

First thing to try

  1. Look in the Properties section below where it says State: Todo.

  2. Click it and change it to Done from the dropdown. Or drag this card to the Done column in Board view.

That's the same UI you'll use to track every agent run from here on.

name: 1. Connect a repo to your project 🔗
description_html:
A Project in Pi Dash is the container for the repo your agents will work in. The repository URL is set once at project creation, and runners use it to clone the code into a working tree on first run.

Note: This tutorial project has no repo attached — it's read-only walkthrough content. Create a fresh project pointed at one of your real git repositories to run agents for real.

Try it:

  1. In the left sidebar, hover over Projects and click the + icon.

  2. Fill in Name, Identifier, Repository URL (e.g. git@github.com:acme/web.git), and Default branch (usually main).

  3. Click Create. Runners on your machine will clone this repo the first time they pick up a run, using whatever git credentials are already on the host (SSH keys, etc.).

Tip: Keep one project per repository. Every run for that project then shares the same working tree on the runner — no surprise crossover between unrelated codebases. Press P from anywhere to open the new-project modal.

Runners will clone this repo the first time they pick up a run. Tip: Keep one project per repository. Press P from anywhere to open the new-project modal.

name: 2. Install pidash & register a runner 💻
description_html:
A Runner is a small daemon that runs on your own machine. It picks up work assigned to it from Pi Dash, dispatches each run to the AI agent you've installed locally (Claude Code or Codex), streams progress back, and surfaces approval requests when the agent wants to do something sensitive.

Install the pidash CLI

Full install commands for macOS, Linux, and Windows live in the quickstart docs. In short:

  1. Install pidash (one-line installer for macOS/Linux, MSI for Windows).

  2. Install an agent: Claude Code (verify with claude --version) or Codex (verify with codex --version).

  3. Run pidash auth login --url <your-instance-url> and approve the device code in your browser.

  4. Find your project ID under Project → Settings → General, then run pidash runner add --project <PROJECT_ID>. This installs an OS service (systemd, launchd, or Task Scheduler) bound to that project.

  5. Run pidash doctor. It must be clean before continuing.

Note: One host can run multiple runners — one per project. Each runner only sees code inside its own working directory.

Tip: pidash status shows daemon + runner state at any time. pidash tui opens an interactive dashboard.

It picks up work assigned to it from Pi Dash, dispatches each run to the AI agent you've installed locally (Claude Code or Codex), streams progress back, and surfaces approval requests when the agent wants to do something sensitive. Install the pidash CLI: full install commands for macOS, Linux, and Windows live in the quickstart docs. In short: install pidash, install an agent (Claude Code or Codex), run pidash auth login --url <your-instance-url>, find your project ID under Project → Settings → General, then run pidash runner add --project <PROJECT_ID>. Finally, pidash doctor must be clean before continuing. One host can run multiple runners — one per project. Tip: pidash status and pidash tui are your friends.

name: 3. Write a great work item for your agent ✏️
description_html:
A work item is the unit of work you hand to an agent. The agent reads the title and description as its prompt — so unlike a ticket written for a human, vague phrasing produces vague results.

A good agent work item has

  • A clear, specific title — describe one change in one line.

  • A description with context — relevant file paths, the constraint, the behavior to change, links to related work.

  • Explicit acceptance criteria — what "done" looks like. Tests passing, an endpoint returning X, a button doing Y.

Try it:

  1. Click Add work item on the Work Items page.

  2. Write a title scoped to a single change. Skip "Improve auth." Try "Reject login when email contains uppercase before the normalize step."

  3. In the description, paste the relevant file paths, related error logs, and any constraints. The agent reads what you give it — assume zero prior context.

  4. Set priority and assignees if useful — these are signals for the humans watching, not for the agent.

Tip: Press C from anywhere in your project to open the new-work-item modal.

Want to dive deeper? See the agent orchestration docs.
description_stripped: A work item is the unit of work you hand to an agent. The agent reads the title and description as its prompt — so unlike a ticket for a human, vague phrasing produces vague results. A good agent work item has: a clear, specific title (describe one change in one line); a description with context (file paths, constraints, behavior to change, related links); explicit acceptance criteria (what 'done' looks like — tests passing, an endpoint returning X, a button doing Y). Try it: click Add work item, write a tightly scoped title, paste relevant file paths and logs in the description, set priority and assignees as usual. Tip: press C from anywhere to open the new-work-item modal.

name: 4. Click "Run AI" and watch the agent work 🤖
description_html:
Once a work item describes what you want, hand it to an agent.

Try it:

  1. Open any work item.

  2. Click the Run AI button at the top of the right-hand sidebar (purple AI icon).

  3. The matcher picks an eligible runner on your machine, ships the prompt, and the agent starts work.

What happens next

A Run progresses through phases — typically planning, coding, and review — each with its own prompt template. Watch it live in two places:

  • The work item's Run panel — phase progress, agent transcript, current step.

  • The Runners → Runs tab — every run across the workspace, with status.

Run statuses

queued → assigned → running → awaiting_approval / completed / failed / cancelled

Tip: A single work item can have many runs. Re-running after a failure, or commenting and then re-running with new context, is normal — every attempt is preserved.

name: 5. Approve agent actions safely 🛡️
description_html:
Every agent run goes through a "first-writer-wins" approval router. When the agent wants to do something sensitive — execute a shell command, write a file, or fetch a URL — it pauses and asks. Nothing risky happens until someone (or a configured policy) says yes.

Where approvals appear

  • Inline in the work item's Run panel.

  • In Runners → Approvals — a centralized queue of all pending approvals across the workspace.

  • In pidash tui on the runner host — handy when you're already in a terminal.

Three decisions

  • Approve once — let this one action through. The next similar action will ask again.

  • Approve for session — trust this kind of action for the rest of the run.

  • Decline — block the action. The agent decides whether to retry differently or stop.

Note: Decisions race across surfaces. If you approve from the TUI and a teammate declines in the web UI a moment later, the first decision wins.

Approval kinds you'll see: command_execution, file_change, network_access.
description_stripped: Every agent run goes through a 'first-writer-wins' approval router. When the agent wants to do something sensitive — execute a shell command, write a file, or fetch a URL — it pauses and asks. Nothing risky happens until someone (or a configured policy) says yes. Where approvals appear: inline in the work item's Run panel, in Runners → Approvals (the centralized queue), or in pidash tui on the runner host. Three decisions: Approve once, Approve for session, Decline. Decisions race across surfaces — the first writer wins. Approval kinds you'll see: command_execution, file_change, network_access.

name: 6. Review the diff & iterate 🔍
description_html:
A finished run leaves three things behind:

  • A diff of the files the agent touched in the runner's working tree.

  • A full transcript of the agent's steps — every tool call and every approval.

  • A done-signal payload — the structured summary the agent emits when it believes the work item is complete.

Try it:

  1. Open the work item once the run reaches the completed state.

  2. Read the diff and transcript in the Run panel.

  3. To commit the changes: pidash status shows the runner's working-directory path. cd there and use git as normal.

When it's not right

  • Add a comment to the work item with what to fix, then click Run AI again — the next run will pick up the new context.

  • If the agent went off track mid-run, cancel it and re-run.

  • Every run is preserved, so you can compare attempts side by side.

That's the loop: work item → Run AI → approve → review → iterate.

You're done with the tour. Delete or archive this tutorial project whenever you're ready — it's just a guided demo and isn't load-bearing.
